name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ لَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُكُم مِّن كِتَـٰبٍ وَحِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِۦ وَلَتَنصُرُنَّهُۥ ۚ قَالَ ءَأَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَخَذْتُمْ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ إِصْرِى ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَقْرَرْنَا ۚ قَالَ فَٱشْهَدُوا۟ وَأَنَا۠ مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٨١)
```

```english
When Allah took a compact concerning the prophets, [He said,] ‘Inasmuch as I have given you [the knowledge] of the Book and wisdom, should an apostle come to you thereafter confirming what is with you, you shall believe in him and help him.’ He said, ‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’ They said, ‘We pledge.’ He said, ‘Then be witnesses, and I, too, am among the witnesses along with you.’ (81)
```

```arabic
فَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
Then whoever turns away after that—it is they who are the transgressors. (82)
```

```arabic
أَفَغَيْرَ دِينِ ٱللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ وَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلَمَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ (٨٣)
```

```english
Do they seek a religion other than that of Allah, while to Him submits whoever there is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly, and to Him they will be brought back? (83)
```

```arabic
قُلْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (٨٤)
```

```english
Say, ‘We have faith in Allah and in what has been sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob and the Tribes, and that which Moses, Jesus and the prophets were given by their Lord. We make no distinction between any of them, and to Him do we submit.’ (84)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٨٥)
```

```english
Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter. (85)
```

 thought
## (Commentary)

The verses are not devoid of connection to what preceded them; the context is a single, continuous flow maintaining its unity. It is as if He, the Exalted, having explained that the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) persisted in seeking transgression regarding the knowledge of the Book they were entrusted with—

And the religion (الدين), by distorting words from their proper places, seeking to deceive people by obscuring the matter, differentiating between the Prophets, and denying the signs of the prophethood of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم); and having negated that any Prophet among the Prophets, such as Moses and Jesus (عليهما السلام), would command them to take himself or anyone else from among the Prophets and Angels as lords (أرباباً), contrary to the explicit claims of the Christians and the apparent claims of the Jews—

He intensified the denunciation against them regarding this. How could such a thing occur when Allah took the covenant (الميثاق) from the Prophets that they must believe in every Prophet who comes to them, whether preceding them or succeeding them, and that they must aid him? This is achieved by each of them confirming those Prophets who preceded him and giving glad tidings of those who would succeed him—such as Jesus (عليه السلام) confirming Moses and his law, and giving glad tidings of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). Likewise, He, the Exalted, took the covenant from them to take a pledge (العهد) to that effect from their nations and made them witnesses over them. He explained that this is Islam (الإسلام) — *the total submission to Allah* — whose authority encompasses everyone in the heavens and the earth.

Then He commanded His Prophet to proceed upon this covenant in a manner of acceptance and obedience, believing in Allah and in all that He revealed to His Prophets without differentiating between them, and to submit (يسلم) to Allah, the Glorified, and to represent himself and his nation in this. This is the meaning of taking the covenant from him directly and from his nation through him, as the explanation will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة ثم جاءكم رسول مصدق لما معكم لتؤمنن به ولتنصرنه** **"When Allah took the covenant of the prophets: ‘Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom, and then there comes to you an apostle confirming what is with you, you shall surely believe in him and help him.’" (3:81)** The verse informs of a covenant that was taken. Allah took this covenant for the benefit of the Prophets, as indicated by His saying: **ثم جاءكم رسول** "and then there comes to you an apostle..."; just as He, the Exalted, took it from the Prophets, as indicated by His saying: **أأقررتم وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري** "‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’" and His subsequent saying: **قل آمنا بالله** "Say, ‘We believe in Allah...’" (3:84). Thus, the covenant is one taken for the Prophets and taken from them, even if it was also taken from others through their mediation.

Accordingly, it is permissible that "the covenant of the Prophets" (ميثاق النبيين) refers to the covenant taken from them or the one taken for them, as the covenant is one and the same. In other words, "the Prophets" could refer to those for whom the covenant was taken and those from whom it was taken. However, the context of His saying: **ما كان لبشر أن يؤتيه الله** "It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him..." (3:79) until the end of the two verses, in its connection to this verse, supports the view that "the Prophets" refers to those from whom the covenant was taken. The unity of context implies that after Allah gave the Prophets the Book, judgment, and prophethood, it is inconceivable for them to call toward a partner [with Allah]. How could they do so when the covenant was taken from them to believe in and aid other Prophets who call toward the monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — of Allah, the Glorified? Thus, it is more appropriate to begin by mentioning the covenant in terms of its being taken from the Prophets.

Regarding His saying: **لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة** "Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom," the famous recitation—which is the recitation of all except Hamzah—is with a fatha on the *lam* and without doubling (تخفيف) in **لما**. According to this, *ma* (ما) is a relative pronoun (موصولة) and **آتيتكم** (and it has been recited as **آتيناكم**) is its relative clause (صلته). The pronoun [referring back to the relative pronoun] is omitted, indicated by His saying: **من كتاب وحكمة**. The relative pronoun is the subject (مبتدأ), and its predicate (خبر) is His saying: **لتؤمنن به** "you shall surely believe in him...". The *lam* in **لما** is the introductory *lam* (ابتدائية), and the *lam* in **لتؤمنن به** is the *lam* of the oath (لام القسم). The whole phrase is an explanation of the covenant taken. The meaning is: "For that which I have given you of Book and wisdom, and then there comes to you a messenger confirming what is with you, you shall certainly believe in him and aid him."

It is also possible that *ma* (ما) is conditional (شرطية) and its apodosis (جزاؤها) is His saying **لتؤمنن به**. The meaning would be: "Whatever I give you of Book and wisdom, and then there comes to you a messenger confirming what is with you, you must believe in him and aid him." This is better because the entry of the *lam* (whose oath is implied) into the apodosis is more common, the meaning is smoother and clearer, and the conditional form is more familiar in the context of covenants. As for the recitation with a kasra on the *lam* in **لما**, the *lam* there is for causation (للتعليل) and *ma* is a relative pronoun; however, the recitation with fatha is preferred.

The address in His saying **آتيتكم** "I give you" and **جاءكم** "comes to you," although appearing at first glance to be directed at the Prophets, is qualified by His subsequent saying: **أأقررتم وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري** "‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’" This serves as a context (قرينة) that the address is to the Prophets and their nations together. That is, the address is specific to them, but its ruling encompasses them and all their nations. Thus, it is incumbent upon the nations to believe and aid, just as it is incumbent upon the Prophets to believe and aid.

The apparent meaning of His saying: **ثم جاءكم رسول مصدق لما معكم** "and then there comes to you an apostle confirming what is with you," implies a temporal sequence (التراخي الزماني); meaning that the preceding Prophet must believe in and aid the succeeding Prophet. As for what appears from His saying: **قل آمنا بالله** "Say, ‘We believe in Allah...’" (3:84)—that the covenant is taken from both the predecessor and the successor for one another, and that the successor must believe in and aid the predecessor just as the reverse is true—this is a matter sensed through the implication (فحوى) of the address rather than the literal wording of the verse, as will be explained, Allah the Mighty willing.

Regarding His saying: **لتؤمنن به ولتنصرنه** "you shall surely believe in him and help him," although it is possible for the first pronoun [in "believe in him"] to refer to the Messenger just like the second pronoun—since there is no harm in a Prophet believing in another Prophet, as the Exalted said: **آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَمَلَائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ** "The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful. Each [of them] has faith in Allah, His angels, His scriptures and His apostles..." (2:285)—

However, what is apparent from His saying: **قل آمنا بالله وبما أُنزل علينا وما أُنزل علی إبراهيم** "Say, ‘We believe in Allah and what was sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham...’" (3:84) is that it refers back to what they were given of the Book and wisdom, while the second pronoun refers to the Messenger. The meaning would be: "You shall surely believe in what I have given you of Book and wisdom, and you shall surely aid the Messenger who comes to you confirming what is with you."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قال أأقررتم وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري قالوا أقررنا** **"He said, ‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’ They said, ‘We pledge.’" (3:81)** The interrogative is for affirmation (للتقرير). Acknowledgment (الإقرار) is well-known. The *isr* (الإصر) is the pledge or covenant (العهد), and it is the object of "you accept" (أخذتم). Accepting a covenant necessitates a party from whom it is taken other than the one taking it, and these are none other than the nations of the Prophets. Thus, the meaning is: "Do you acknowledge the covenant, and have you taken My pledge from your nations?" They said: "We acknowledge."

It has been said that "accepting the pledge" refers to the Prophets accepting it for themselves. In this case, His saying **وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري** "and accept My covenant on this condition" would be an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for His saying **أأقررتم** "Do you pledge." This is supported by their reply **قالوا أقررنا** "They said, ‘We pledge’" without mentioning "accepting" in the response. According to this, the covenant would not extend beyond the Prophets to their nations. However, this is rendered unlikely by His saying: **قال فاشهدوا** "He said, ‘Then bear witness...’" because witnessing (الشهادة) apparently occurs against others. Likewise, His subsequent saying: **قل آمنا بالله** "Say, ‘We believe in Allah...’" without saying "Say, ‘I believe’" (قل آمنت) suggests that it is the faith of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) on behalf of himself and his nation. Unless it is said that the participation of the nations with the Prophets is only inferred from these two phrases—namely His saying **فاشهدوا** and **قل آمنا بالله**—without His saying **وأخذتم** providing any indication of that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قال فاشهدوا وأنا معكم من الشاهدين** **"He said, ‘Then bear witness, and I am with you among the witnesses.’" (3:81)** The apparent meaning of witnessing, as mentioned, is that it be against others. Thus, it is a witnessing by the Prophets and their nations together. This is supported, as mentioned, by His saying: **قل آمنا بالله**. It is also supported by the context, as the verses are structured to argue against the People of the Book for their failure to respond to the call of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), just as they argue against them regarding what they attributed to Jesus, Moses (عليهما السلام), and others, as indicated by His saying: **أفغير دين الله يبغون** "Do they seek a religion other than Allah’s...?" (3:83) and other verses.

It is sometimes said that the meaning of **فأشهدوا** "Then bear witness" is the witnessing of some Prophets over others. It is also said that those addressed by **فاشهدوا** are the Angels rather than the Prophets.

While both meanings are possible in themselves, the wording does not clearly indicate either of them without a context (قرينة), and you have seen that the context points to the contrary.

Among the subtle points (اللطائف) occurring in the verse is that the covenant is taken from the Prophets for the sake of the Messengers (الرسل), as suggested by His saying: **وإذ أخذنا من النبيين** "And when We took from the prophets..." until His saying: **ثم جاءكم رسول** "and then there comes to you an apostle...". It was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying: **كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً** "Mankind were a single community..." (2:213) the difference between prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة), and that the Messenger (الرسول) is more specific in its instance than the Prophet (النبي).

Thus, according to the apparent meaning of the wording, the covenant is taken from the station of prophethood for the station of messengership, without indicating the reverse.

By this, one may critique what some have mentioned: that the summary of the verse's meaning is that the covenant was taken from all Prophets to confirm one another and command belief in one another—meaning that the religion is one, to which all Prophets call. This is obvious.

The summary of the verse's meaning, based on what has preceded, is: Allah took the covenant from the Prophets and their nations that if Allah gave them the Book and wisdom and a Messenger came to them confirming what was with them, they must believe in what He gave them and aid the Messenger. From the Prophets, this entails the successor confirming the predecessor and contemporary, and the predecessor giving glad tidings of the successor and instructing the nation. From the nation, it entails faith, confirmation, and aid. The necessary consequence of this is the unity of the divine religion.

As for what some exegetes mentioned—that the meaning of the verse is that Allah took the covenant from the Prophets to confirm Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and give glad tidings to their nations of his advent—while this is correct, it is a matter indicated by the context of the verses as previously noted, rather than the verse in itself due to the generality of the wording. Rather, it is inferred from the verse's occurrence within the argument against the People of the Book and the blame and reproach directed at them for their preoccupation with distorting their books, concealing the signs of prophethood, and their stubbornness and rebellion against the explicit truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فمن تولی بعد ذلك** **"Then whoever turns away after this..." (3:82)** is an emphasis of the aforementioned covenant taken, and the meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أفغير دين الله يبغون وله أسلم** **"Do they seek a religion other than Allah’s, while to Him submits...?" (3:83)** is a derivation from the preceding verse involving the taking of the covenant from the Prophets. The meaning is: If the religion of Allah is one—and it is that upon which the covenant was taken from all Prophets and their nations—and it was incumbent upon the preceding Prophets and nations to give glad tidings of the succeeding Messenger and believe in what he brings and confirm him, then what do these People of the Book intend when they have disbelieved in you? Their apparent state is that they seek religion; do they then seek other than Islam, which is the only religion of Allah?

Therefore, they do not believe you nor do they hold fast to the religion of Islam, even though it was mandatory for them to seek refuge in Islam because it is the religion based upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. Religion must be so. The proof for this is that whoever is in the heavens and the earth among those possessed of intellect and perception are submissive (مسلمون) to Allah in the realm of creation (التكوين); thus, they must submit to Him in the realm of legislation (التشريع).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وله أسلم من في السموات والأرض طوعاً وكرها** **"while to Him submits whoever is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly" (3:83).** This submission (الإسلام) encompasses whoever is in the heavens and the earth, including the People of the Book who are mentioned as not being submissive. The word **أسلم** (submitted) is in the past tense, apparently indicating past occurrence and certain realization. This is the existential submission (التسليم التكويني) to the command of Allah, rather than Islam in the sense of devotional humility (الخضوع العبودي). This is supported or indicated by His saying: **طوعاً وكرهاً** "willingly or unwillingly."

Accordingly, His saying **وله أسلم** is a case of sufficing with the mention of the proof and cause instead of the thing proven and the effect. The implied meaning is: "Do they seek other than Islam? It is the religion of Allah because whoever is in the heavens and the earth is submissive to Him, yielding to His command. If they are pleased with it, their yielding is willing (طوعاً) from themselves; and if they dislike what He willed and desired something else, the command remains His command and it occurs upon them unwillingly (كرهاً) without their volition."

From here it becomes clear that the "and" (الواو) in **طوعاً وكرهاً** is for division (للتقسيم), and that "willingly" and "unwillingly" refer to their pleasure with what Allah willed for them of what they love, and their dislike for what He willed for them of what they do not love, such as death, poverty, illness, and the like.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وإليه يرجعون** **"and to Him they will be returned" (3:83).** This is another reason for the obligation of seeking Islam as a religion, for their return is to Allah, their true Master, not to that toward which their disbelief and polytheism (الشرك) — *the association of partners with Allah* — lead them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل آمنا بالله وما أُنزل علينا** **"Say, ‘We believe in Allah and what was sent down to us...’" (3:84).** The Prophet was commanded to proceed upon the covenant taken from him and others, saying on behalf of himself and the believers of his nation: "We believe in Allah and what was sent down to us..."

This is among the evidences that the covenant was taken from the Prophets and their nations together, as alluded to just now.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وما أُنزل علی إبراهيم وإسماعيل** **"and what was sent down to Abraham and Ishmael..." (3:84)** until the end of the verse. Those mentioned by name are the Prophets from the progeny of Abraham. The verse is not devoid of an indication that the "Tribes" (الأسباط) refers to the Prophets from the progeny of Jacob or from the tribes of the Children of Israel, such as David, Solomon, Jonah, Job, and others.

His saying: **والنبيون من ربهم** "and what was given to the prophets from their Lord" (3:84) generalizes the discourse to include Adam, Noah, and those besides them. Then He grouped them all by saying: **لا نفرق بين أحد منهم ونحن له مسلمون** "we make no distinction between any of them, and to Him do we submit."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ومن يبتغ غير الإسلام ديناً فلن يقبل منه** **"Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him..." (3:85)** is a negation of anything other than the case established by the covenant taken. In it is an emphasis on the obligation of proceeding upon the covenant.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, narrated from Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him): "Allah took the covenant from the Prophets before our Prophet that they should inform their nations of his advent and his description, give them glad tidings of him, and command them to confirm him."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him), who said: "Allah did not send any Prophet, from Adam onwards, except that He took from him a pledge (العهد) regarding Muhammad: that if he were sent while the Prophet was alive, he must believe in him and aid him; and He commanded him to take the pledge from his people." Then he recited: **وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة** "When Allah took the covenant of the prophets: ‘Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom...’"

**I say:** The two narrations explain the verse through the totality of what the wording and context indicate, as previously mentioned.

In *al-Majma'* and *al-Jawami'*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "Its meaning is: 'When Allah took the covenant of the nations of the Prophets, every nation to confirm its Prophet and act upon what he brought them.' But they did not fulfill it; they abandoned many of their laws and distorted much."

**I say:** What is mentioned in the narration is of the nature of mentioning an instance (مصداق) to which the verse applies; thus, it does not contradict the fact that the intended meaning of the verse encompasses the Prophets and their nations together.

Also in *al-Majma'*, from Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أأقررتم وأخذتم** "‘Do you pledge and accept...’": "He said: 'Do you acknowledge and have you taken the pledge (العهد) for that from your nations?' The Prophets and their nations said: 'We acknowledge that which You commanded us to acknowledge.' Allah said: 'Then bear witness to that against your nations, and I am with you among the witnesses against you and against your nations.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: **قال فاشهدوا** "He said, ‘Then bear witness...’": "He says: 'Then bear witness against your nations regarding that, and I am with you among the witnesses against you and against them. So whoever turns away from you, O Muhammad, after this pledge from all nations, those are the transgressors (الفاسقون)—they are the ones disobedient in disbelief.'"

**I say:** The justification for the meaning of the narration has already been mentioned.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "He said to them in the [realm of] particles (الذر): **أأقررتم وأخذتم علی ذلكم إصري** 'Do you pledge and accept My covenant (عهدي) on this condition?' They said: 'We pledge.' Allah said to the Angels: 'Then bear witness.'"

**I say:** The wording of the verse does not preclude this, even if it is not inferred from its apparent meaning, as previously stated.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur* regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ومن يبتغ غير الإسلام ديناً** "Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam..." (3:85), Ahmad and al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat* narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Deeds will come on the Day of Resurrection. Prayer will come and say: "O Lord, I am Prayer." He will say: "You are upon good." Charity will come and say: "O Lord, I am Charity." He will say: "You are upon good." Then Fasting will come and say: "I am Fasting." He will say: "You are upon good." Then [other] deeds will come, and for all of them Allah will say: "You are upon good; by you today I take and by you I give."' Allah said in His Book: **ومن يبتغ غير الإسلام ديناً فلن يقبل منه وهو في الآخرة من الخاسرين** 'Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter.'"

In *al-Tawhid* and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi regarding the verse, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "It is their monotheism (توحيدهم) of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

**I say:** The mentioned monotheism necessitates submission (التسليم) in all that Allah, the Exalted, desires from His servants; thus, it returns to the meaning we provided in the commentary.

If it were intended as merely the negation of a partner, then "willingly" and "unwillingly" would refer to voluntary and involuntary indication [of His oneness].

Know that there are several other narrations narrated by al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi in their commentaries and others regarding the meaning of His saying: **وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين** "When Allah took the covenant of the prophets...". In them, it is mentioned: "You shall surely believe in the Messenger of Allah, and you shall surely aid the Commander of the Faithful (عليهما الصلوة والسلام)." Their apparent sense is an interpretation of the verse by referring the pronoun in **لتؤمنن به** "you shall surely believe in him" to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and the pronoun in **ولتنصرنه** "and help him" to the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), without any evidence from the wording to indicate this.

However, in what al-Ayyashi narrated from Sallam ibn al-Mustanir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "They have named themselves with a name that Allah did not name anyone with except Ali ibn Abi Talib, and its esoteric interpretation (تأويله) has not yet come." I said: "May I be your ransom, when will its esoteric interpretation come?" He said: "When it comes, Allah will gather before him the Prophets and the believers so that they may aid him. That is the saying of Allah: **وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة** until His saying: **وأنا معكم من الشاهدين**."

By this, the difficulty is eased. For the objection would only arise if the narrations were intended as exegesis (تفسير); as for esoteric interpretation (التأويل), you have already learned that it is not of the nature of [literal] meaning, nor is it linked to the wording, in what preceded in the commentary of His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ** "It is He who has sent down to you the Book..." (3:7).
